title: DAVID — The Tongue Rome Learned Its Alphabet From

brand.title: DAVID · The Archive
brand.subtitle: Dead languages, actually pronounced.
brand.cta: Bring the dead tongues back — one attestation at a time. DAVID.

beats.speech_text: I am DAVID. Today, Etruscan — the language isolate of ancient Italy, the civilization Rome absorbed and rarely credited, the tongue that gave Latin speakers their alphabet.

We can read Etruscan aloud — the alphabet is clear, the phonetics are largely recoverable. But much of the vocabulary still resists translation, making Etruscan one of the most tantalising partly-understood archives in the world.
So I ask the question I ask of every supposedly dead tongue: who kept speaking it, and what does the corpus preserve about its sound?

The proof is in thirteen thousand inscriptions — mostly funerary, cut into stone and bronze across Etruria — plus the Liber Linteus: the only surviving pre-Roman book written on linen, now wrapped around an Egyptian mummy in a Zagreb museum.

We reconstruct Etruscan corpus-first: bilingual inscriptions with Latin and Punic anchor vocabulary, the Pyrgi Tablets give a three-language overlap, and Etruscan loanwords preserved in Latin confirm sounds the alphabet already suggests.

The phonology is well-recovered; the grammar is partially understood — we recognise case endings and verb forms. But much vocabulary remains untranslated, and Etruscan has no confirmed linguistic relatives. I label each claim accordingly.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED alphabet · PARTIAL vocabulary · UNKNOWN relatives

beats.speech_text: Listen — mi nunar thamuce velus hulchniesi — I, the gift, was made for Vel Hulchnie — a dedicatory inscription, read with the phonology the Etruscan alphabet makes direct.
beats.on_screen: ATTESTED TEXT · Etruscan · dedicatory inscription, Etruria

beats.speech_text: Follow the Archive, and the language they told you was frozen starts moving again — one attestation at a time.
